summary: McKenzie provides a casual life update after a month-long blogging hiatus. He describes his day job responsibilities managing Indian colleagues visiting Japan, minimal progress on Bingo Card Creator, and early research on a next project. He offers a pointed aside about Japan's engineering shortage, attributing it to poor pay rather than cultural factors. Most of his free time has been consumed by a Japanese video game rather than his software business.
key_points: Japan's engineering shortage is better explained by economics ($24k starting salary, 12-hour days) than by cultural malaise
As the only English speaker at his company besides the president, McKenzie is drafted into management responsibilities by default
Side businesses stall when day job demands and personal life consume available energy and time
money_quotes: I have a simpler explanation: there is no pay premium for engineering over a normal college degree, which means that the starting salary for a computer programmer is about $24,000, and you're more or less required to work 12 hour days at a minimum.
Given that I'm the only person at the English who speaks English and doesn't answer to "Mr. President", it looks like I'm going to end up being their manager one way or another.
I'm torn by a desire to be supportive when she says "I was grading homework until 6:30 last night!" and a desire to remind her that I'm a Japanese salaryman and that 6:30 is practically a day off.
So basically, when I get home at midnight and have two hours to kill before going to bed, its generally been here instead of the ISV.
Bingo Card Creator — Answering emails and not a whole lot else.
key_insight: Japan's engineering talent shortage is fundamentally an economic problem — when engineering pays no premium over other degrees and demands brutal hours, rational people choose other careers.
